worth_it_explanation: Names are the labels every report, deal, and troubleshooting session reads from, so a naming convention that a person can parse at a glance turns reporting from guesswork into a slice-and-filter exercise. When tag, placement, and deal names follow no shared pattern, a publisher cannot group revenue by site, format, or buyer without reverse-engineering each name by hand. The check is a review of names that already exist in Microsoft Monetize, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete relabeling the publisher can stage without touching delivery.
intro: A naming convention is the thread that ties a publisher's Microsoft Monetize objects to the reports built on top of them. Tag and placement names feed the dimensions a publisher slices revenue by, and deal and package names are how a buyer, a curator, and the publisher all refer to the same transaction. This subcategory is about whether those names follow a consistent, parseable pattern rather than the configuration of the tags and deals themselves. These checks confirm that names carry the structure reporting depends on, so a publisher can group inventory by site, format, geography, or buyer without decoding each name one at a time.

audit_checks.title: Ad tag and placement names do not follow a consistent, parseable convention
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When tag and placement names follow no shared pattern, reporting cannot group revenue by site, format, geography, or position without someone decoding each name by hand. The data is all present, but it cannot be sliced, so the publisher loses the ability to see which inventory performs and which does not until the names are reworked.

educate.base:
In Microsoft Monetize, the placement is the object that links a slot on the page to the inventory a publisher offers for sale, and the tag is the code that calls that placement. The name a publisher gives each one is not cosmetic. It is the label every report reads from when it groups revenue, fill, and eCPM by inventory, so the structure inside the name decides what a publisher can later slice by.

A parseable name encodes the attributes that matter for reporting in a fixed order, so a person and a filter can both read them. A name like 'siteA_articlefooter_300x250_desktop' says which site, which position, which size, and which device in one string, and every tag built the same way can be grouped on any of those fields. A name like 'newtag2' or 'test_final_v3' says nothing a report can group on, so the revenue under it sits in a bucket no one can break apart.

The cost is not lost data. Microsoft Monetize records the impressions and revenue either way. The cost is lost sliceability. When names share no pattern, a publisher who wants revenue by format, or fill by device, or eCPM by site has to reverse-engineer each name before the report means anything, and a name that encodes nothing cannot be reverse-engineered at all. The header-bidding mechanics behind Microsoft Monetize lean on the same idea, where Prebid's own guidance frames a stable, descriptive label per slot as the thing that makes fine-grained reporting and deal targeting possible.

A few signs point to tag and placement names that are not parseable:
  • Names use sequential or throwaway labels, e.g., 'tag_1', 'newunit', or 'test_copy', that carry no attribute a report can group on
  • The same attribute appears in different orders or spellings across names, e.g., 'desktop' in one and 'dsk' in another, so a filter cannot catch them all
  • No written convention exists that says which attributes a name must carry and in what order
You may want to confirm that tag and placement names follow one written convention that encodes the attributes reporting needs. One approach is to settle on a fixed field order, e.g., site, position, size, then device, and bring existing names onto it as inventory is touched rather than in one disruptive pass.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site with many sections and templates, the same ad position repeats across the homepage, section fronts, and article pages, so a name that encodes section and template lets a publisher compare the same slot across contexts. You may want the convention to carry the section or page type as a field so reporting can show how an article-footer unit performs against a homepage unit of the same size.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, product, category, and editorial pages carry different audience intent, so a name that encodes page type lets a publisher separate the commercial inventory from the editorial inventory in reporting. You may want the convention to carry page type as a field so the higher-intent product and category placements can be grouped apart from editorial.

learn_more.source_title: Prebid Ad Slot and GPID for Header Bidding

investigate.intro: Review the tag and placement names across the inventory and look for whether they follow one written convention that encodes the attributes reporting needs.

monetize_ui.label: Microsoft Monetize console
steps: Open the inventory section and list the active placements with their names.
Read the names for a shared pattern, and record whether each encodes attributes such as site, position, size, and device, or whether names are sequential or throwaway labels.
Note any case where the same attribute appears in different orders or spellings across names, which would break a report filter built on it.
Ask the publisher whether a written naming convention exists, and record what it specifies and whether the live names follow it.

monetize_reporting.label: Microsoft Monetize reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by placement, with revenue, impressions, and eCPM.
Try to group the report by an attribute such as format or device using the placement name alone, and record whether the names support that grouping or whether each must be decoded by hand.
Note any inventory whose revenue cannot be grouped because its name encodes nothing a report can read.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether a written naming convention exists and whether the live tag and placement names follow it, encoding the attributes reporting groups by in a consistent order. Names that follow one convention and let reporting group by site, format, geography, or device pass. Names that are sequential or throwaway, or that spell or order the same attribute inconsistently, are a fail recorded with the placements affected and the attributes that cannot be sliced.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether a naming convention exists and which placements follow it above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that tag and placement names on {{site_name}} do not follow a consistent, parseable convention, so reporting cannot group revenue by site, format, geography, or device without decoding each name by hand. Microsoft Monetize records the impressions and revenue either way, but a name that encodes nothing cannot be sliced, so the publisher loses the ability to see which inventory performs. {{voice_recommend}} settling on one written convention with a fixed field order, e.g., site, position, size, then device, and bringing existing names onto it as inventory is touched rather than in one disruptive pass. After the relabeling, confirm that a report can group inventory on each encoded attribute from the names alone.

audit_checks.title: Deal and package names are inconsistent or not identifiable
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A deal name is the shared reference a publisher, a buyer, and any curator use for the same transaction, so a name that does not identify the buyer, the inventory, or the terms forces every party to confirm what a deal covers before acting on it. When deal names are inconsistent, a publisher cannot reconcile which deal underdelivered or troubleshoot a deal that returns no revenue because the name does not tell them what it was meant to do.

educate.base:
A deal in Microsoft Monetize is a private agreement between a publisher and a buyer that reserves inventory at agreed terms, and a package groups inventory a publisher offers to a buyer. The name on each is the reference all parties share. A publisher reads it in reporting, a buyer matches it against the deal identifier in their buying platform, and a curator who assembled the deal refers to it by the same string. When that name is consistent and identifiable, every party reads the same meaning from it.

An identifiable deal name carries the attributes that distinguish one deal from another, so a name like 'BuyerX_RunOfSite_Q1_PreferredCPM' tells a reader the buyer, the inventory, the period, and the deal type without opening the deal. A name like 'Deal 4' or 'new deal copy' tells a reader nothing, so reconciling which deal underdelivered, or troubleshooting a deal that returns no revenue, starts with opening each deal to learn what it was meant to do.

The cost shows up at reconciliation and at troubleshooting. A publisher comparing booked terms against delivered revenue needs to match each deal in their report to the agreement it represents, and a name that does not identify the buyer or the terms breaks that match. A deal that configures the right inventory yet delivers little revenue is a known failure mode in programmatic deals, and an unidentifiable name makes that deal harder to find and harder to diagnose. The IAB Tech Lab's Deals API specification exists to standardize how a deal is identified across the parties that package and sell it, which is the same consistency a publisher's own deal names should carry internally.

A few signs point to deal and package names that are not identifiable:
  • Names are sequential or throwaway, e.g., 'Deal 4' or 'test deal', and carry no buyer, inventory, or term attribute
  • The same buyer or deal type is named differently across deals, so reporting cannot group a buyer's deals together
  • No written convention exists that says which attributes a deal or package name must carry
You may want to confirm that deal and package names identify the buyer, the inventory, and the deal terms in a consistent pattern. One approach is to agree on a field order, e.g., buyer, inventory, period, then deal type, and apply it to new deals while relabeling active deals at renewal.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a publisher often runs several deals with the same buyer across different sections or seasons, so a name that encodes the buyer and the period keeps those deals distinct in reporting. You may want the convention to carry the buyer and the inventory scope as fields so a buyer's homepage deal and section deal can be told apart at a glance.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, deals tied to product or category inventory carry different value than editorial deals, so a name that encodes the inventory scope separates the commercial deals from the editorial ones in reporting. You may want the convention to carry the inventory scope as a field so reconciliation can group the higher-value commercial deals on their own.

learn_more.source_title: Prebid.js and Ad Server Key Values for Header Bidding

investigate.intro: Review the deal and package names against the agreements they represent and look for whether they identify the buyer, the inventory, and the terms in a consistent pattern.

monetize_ui.label: Microsoft Monetize console
steps: Open the deals section and list the active deals and packages with their names.
For each, read whether the name identifies the buyer, the inventory scope, the period, and the deal type, or whether it is a sequential or throwaway label.
Record any case where the same buyer or deal type is named differently across deals, which would prevent grouping a buyer's deals in reporting.
Ask the publisher whether a written naming convention exists for deals and packages, and record what it specifies and whether the live names follow it.

monetize_reporting.label: Microsoft Monetize reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by deal, with revenue, impressions, and eCPM.
Try to group the report by buyer or deal type using the deal name alone, and record whether the names support that grouping or whether each must be matched to its agreement by hand.
Note any deal that returns little or no revenue and check whether its name identifies the inventory and terms well enough to begin troubleshooting.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether a written naming convention exists for deals and packages and whether the live names follow it, identifying the buyer, the inventory, and the terms consistently. Names that identify the deal and let reporting group by buyer or deal type pass. Names that are sequential or throwaway, or that name the same buyer or deal type inconsistently, are a fail recorded with the deals affected and what cannot be reconciled or grouped.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether a deal naming convention exists and which deals follow it above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that deal and package names on {{site_name}} are inconsistent or do not identify the buyer, the inventory, or the terms, so reconciling which deal underdelivered and troubleshooting a deal that returns no revenue both start with opening each deal to learn what it was meant to do. A deal name is the shared reference a publisher, a buyer, and any curator use for the same transaction, and a name that identifies nothing breaks that match. {{voice_recommend}} agreeing on one convention with a fixed field order, e.g., buyer, inventory, period, then deal type, applying it to new deals and relabeling active deals at renewal. After the change, confirm that a report can group deals by buyer and deal type from the names alone.
